A research group is trying to understand how a system could improve itself by generating ideas and then judging them. They accept that this sounds plausible only if the judging part is more stable than the generating part. But they cannot explain why that stability should exist. So the first consequence is simple: the group cannot confidently treat self-judgment as a reliable engine of recursive improvement. They can imagine the mechanism, but they cannot ground it.

They also consider a metric like “289 unique words” as a sign of novelty. But they accept that this may not matter, because a theory like relativity may owe its originality mostly to new relations among already familiar concepts. So lexical variety cannot be assumed to track conceptual innovation. The group therefore loses a convenient surface indicator. If the generator produces unusual vocabulary, that may mean nothing. If it uses ordinary vocabulary, that may still conceal a major advance. Evaluation becomes harder, not easier.

This pressure falls back onto the judging function. Yet that function was already unexplained in its alleged stability. So the system faces a compound problem: it needs a robust evaluator precisely where easy proxies for novelty are distrusted.

Now memory enters. The architecture may need forgetting, presumably to prevent overload from accumulated candidates. But forgetting is risky, because ideas discarded under one set of constraints may become valuable under another. Therefore any forgetting mechanism trades tractability against recoverability. If it forgets aggressively, it may erase the path to later breakthroughs. If it forgets conservatively, it may drown in its own search history. Since the evaluator is not securely grounded, it is also not obvious that the system can tell what is safe to forget.

The last two premises introduce outputs: “Jangles misemploy cytotaxonomically solemnified unelided” and “Understrapper assoiling unbethink existentialistically spogel.” These sentences contain unusual words and relations. Given the earlier premises, the group cannot infer much from that fact alone. The strange vocabulary does not establish novelty in the important sense. Nor does it establish uselessness. If novelty lives in relations among familiar concepts, these utterances may be irrelevant noise; but without a stable evaluator, the system cannot decisively prove that either. They are candidates in memory, costly to keep, risky to discard.

So the world implied by all the premises is one in which generation can produce exotic strings, but the criteria for distinguishing progress from noise are underjustified. Lexical novelty is not trusted. Forgetting is necessary but dangerous. Self-improvement by self-judgment remains conceptually possible yet operationally unsupported, because the one thing it most requires—a more stable evaluator than generator—has not been explained.
